image.title: Latest & Breaking News on Fox News

item.title: WATCH: Pelosi dodges questions on husband's new hit-and-run charge
item.description: Nancy Pelosi refused to answer questions about Paul Pelosi's misdemeanor hit-and-run charge and her vote to slash $3.3 billion in aid to Israel.
item.content:encoded:
Former Speaker of the House Nancy Pelosi, D-Calif., remained silent when repeatedly pressed about her husband's newly filed misdemeanor hit-and-run charge, declining to answer whether she believed the charge was fair.

"Can you tell us if you think the misdemeanor charge against Paul was fair?" Fox News Digital asked Pelosi as she walked to her car, refusing to respond.

Paul Pelosi faces a maximum sentence of up to six months in county jail, a fine of up to $1,000 or both. In addition to the misdemeanor filed Friday, Pelosi was also charged with an infraction for making an unsafe turn in the incident on July 3.

PAUL PELOSI, 86, FACES A HIT-AND-RUN CHARGE AFTER STRIKING A PARKED CAR IN NAPA COUNTY: SHERIFF'S OFFICE

The charge filed by the Napa County District Attorney’s Office come after the 86-year-old broke California law by allegedly not making any attempt to contact or identify the owner of a parked Tesla that he damaged.

As Pelosi drove away in his Maserati convertible, his car broke down after driving about a half mile. Deputies found Pelosi, and he claimed that he had known he hit something, but was unsure of what and had planned to return to the crash scene.

The incident prompted authorities to request Pelosi undergo a behind-the-wheel driving evaluation to ensure he is able to safely operate a vehicle.

PELOSI STAFFER PUMPS THE BRAKES WHEN PRESSED ON HUSBAND'S HIT-AND-RUN: 'THAT'S ENOUGH'

This is not the first time Pelosi has gained national attention for an unlawful driving incident. In 2022, he pleaded guilty to driving under the influence and crashing into an oncoming Jeep, injuring the driver and was charged with a misdemeanor.

It was also reported by The New York Times that Pelosi was behind the wheel in the 1957 incident that killed his 19-year-old brother, though he was cleared by a jury for any wrongdoing in the case.

HOUSE DEMOCRATS FRACTURE BADLY OVER MASSIE AMENDMENT TO CUT $3.3B IN U.S. AID TO ISRAEL

Fox News Digital also asked the Democratic former speaker about her recent vote — alongside over 100 House Democrats — for an amendment introduced by Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Ky., to cut $3.3 billion in aid to Israel.

The amendment failed 314-104, with Massie being the only Republican to vote in its favor, but it showed the pressure on the Democratic Party to take a stance against Israel and its handling of the war in Gaza.

It also represents the changing view of Democrats’ stance on Israel, particularly under the Trump administration and as Israel’s Prime Minister, Benjamin Netanyahu, has been under fire for his actions toward Gaza.

Primary elections have also reflected American voters’ outrage with Israel, as the nation has seen progressive candidates who have spoken out against Israel and in favor of Palestinians sweep victories over incumbents and mainstream Democrats as the elected nominees for the November midterms.

item.title: Fleeing suspect captured after wild Range Rover chase across Los Angeles County
item.description: Video shows the black Range Rover weaving through traffic on the 101 Freeway before CHP officers rammed the SUV to end the chase in Montebello.
item.content:encoded:
A suspect on the move was taken into custody Tuesday morning after leading California Highway Patrol officers on a high-speed chase through Los Angeles County.

Live video of the chase aired on FOX 11 Los Angeles.

WATCH: FELONY SUSPECT'S OCEAN GETAWAY UNRAVELS AFTER 90-MINUTE STANDOFF WITH LIFEGUARDS

The incident began around 7:15 a.m. when Santa Monica Police Department investigators attempted to take the driver of a black Range Rover, who was allegedly involved in a road rage incident a couple of weeks ago, into custody, according to police.

The driver exited the freeway into Koreatown and continued speeding through surface streets, at times crossing into opposing lanes and endangering morning commuters.

2 COLOMBIAN NATIONALS ARRESTED IN STOLEN MERCEDES LINKED TO VIOLENT CALIFORNIA CARJACKING

The Range Rover reportedly reached speeds exceeding 100 mph as the chase entered the San Gabriel Valley shortly before 8 a.m.

The pursuit ended in Montebello after the driver traveled into a center median and a trailing patrol vehicle rammed the SUV. Multiple officers surrounded the Range Rover and took the suspect into custody, according to the report.

Authorities have not identified the suspect or disclosed details about why he was being pursued.

item.title: Whitney Cummings claims lurid Trump joke was cut from Netflix's Mark Twain Prize special
item.description: Whitney Cummings alleged a joke she made about President Donald Trump was cut by Netflix from its Mark Twain Prize special honoring Bill Maher.
item.content:encoded:
Comedian Whitney Cummings is claiming that one of her jokes about President Donald Trump was cut from Netflix's Mark Twain Prize special honoring Bill Maher.

Cummings, who was among those who paid tribute to the "Real Time" host at the Kennedy Center ceremony last month, made headlines with an edgy jab about the president, telling the audience, "I actually heard Trump may come tonight, but he couldn’t make it. He got caught in sex traffic."

Cummings said her routine was meddled with.

"Not only did I have to cut a joke in rehearsal, then the joke I did — I ended on 'Donald Trump couldn't make it tonight, he got caught in sex traffic' — it stayed in, and then they said, 'We would like to cut it out,'" Cummings said on Saturday's installment of her "Good for You" podcast.

BILL MAHER SAYS IT’S 'SO STUPID' FOR ANTI-TRUMP ARTISTS TO BAIL ON KENNEDY CENTER PERFORMANCES

She clarified the Kennedy Center staff as being "amazing" and said they "did their job," noting that Trump was their boss since he was made chair of the Kennedy Center board.

"I was presented with a tricky conundrum. I just don't know if I did the right thing or not," Cummings said. "It was very clear to me that they would lose their jobs."

"The joke was already leaked by someone that was in the audience... but then they said we need to cut it from the broadcast. And I'm like, 'Well it's already out there. Who cares?' And they're like, 'He will sue you.' Which is like, I don't buy it. I don't buy it, No. 1. No. 2, he might," Cummings chuckled before referencing Trump's infamous 2013 lawsuit against Maher after he joked Trump was the son of an "orangutan."

FEDERAL JUDGE ORDERS TRUMP'S NAME REMOVED FROM KENNEDY CENTER, SAYS ONLY CONGRESS CAN RENAME IT

She said she felt the need to fight for the joke because it was the Mark Twain Prize.

"This is the only time that you shouldn't get censored, because it is about comedians using humor to criticize our elected officials," she said. "So I was in this jam where I was like... I need to fight for this joke because it's the Mark Twain Prize. And this is the time I feel like I have to, but also I don't want anyone losing their job."

Cummings went on to shrug off the potential threat of a Trump lawsuit against her, confidently saying, "I would win."

Netflix pointed the finger at the Trump-controlled Kennedy Center, telling Fox News Digital, "While it’s our preference not to cut jokes from anyone’s set, The Kennedy Center has the contractual right to do so."

"Edits to a show are always made in a collaborative way to produce the best product for broadcast," a spokesperson for The Kennedy Center told Fox News Digital about its partnership with Netflix. 

In a previous podcast, Cummings revealed she was forced to cut another Trump joke from her set, this one pertaining to his "petty" move to add his name to the Kennedy Center and that Trump allegedly "always hated" John F. Kennedy, resulting in a vulgar punchline involving Kennedy's affair with actress Marilyn Monroe.

"I was practicing my jokes and people were like, 'Yeah, we'll get fired if you say that.' I'm like, that is the worst thing you could hear as a comic," Cummings recalled. "I did cut a joke, and I feel the need to just say it."

CLICK HERE FOR THE LATEST MEDIA AND CULTURE NEWS

A Kennedy Center source familiar with the production told Fox News Digital that Cummings' comments have "no validity" and that the center "would never fire anyone for a joke."

They also insist there wasn't foul play when it comes to the Trump joke being cut, saying it's "normal course" for the show to be cut down due to time constraints and that it's "standard practice," pointing to jokes cut last year from its special honoring Conan O'Brien. 

The White House did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's requests for comment.

The Mark Twain Prize special honoring Maher began streaming on Netflix on Tuesday. The "sex traffic" jab did not appear during Cummings' routine.

item.title: 'Godzilla vs. Kong' actress Kaylee Hottle dead at 18
item.description: Kaylee Hottle, whose performance as Jia brought authentic deaf representation to the "Godzilla" franchise, died Tuesday morning at 18 after a tragic car accident.
item.content:encoded:
Kaylee Hottle has died, Fox News Digital can confirm.

The 18-year-old deaf actress, known for her role in "Godzilla vs. Kong," died Tuesday morning in a car accident.

TMZ reported that her father revealed the crash occurred in Maryland during a nearly 23-minute livestream, explaining in American Sign Language that he flew from Texas to claim Kaylee's body.

HOLLYWOOD STARS WHO DIED IN 2026

Joshua Hottle said he was told Kaylee had been involved in a car accident. Officials later informed him that her heart stopped on the way to the hospital.

Before landing her first feature film role, Kaylee appeared in commercials and advocacy projects promoting deaf awareness.

A Virginia native born into a multigenerational deaf family, she quickly became a rising talent and an advocate for greater representation in entertainment.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Hottle landed the role of Jia in "Godzilla vs. Kong" in 2021.

She reprised the role three years later in "Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire," reuniting with the franchise as Jia's connection to Kong remained central to the story. The sequel expanded her character's role and further showcased her signing throughout the film.

Her authentic performance as Jia, a deaf girl who shares a unique bond with Kong, earned widespread praise and helped increase representation of deaf actors in major Hollywood productions.

Though her acting career had only recently begun, she left a lasting impact through her performances and her advocacy for greater inclusion in Tinseltown.

item.title: Top Trump official makes new SAVE Act case to Republicans behind closed doors
item.description: OMB Director Vought pressed House Republicans to back a $95 billion budget bill, signaling spending cuts in a future reconciliation package.
item.content:encoded:
President Donald Trump’s top budget man huddled with House Republicans behind closed doors on Tuesday in a bid to quell a potential conservative mutiny over Speaker Mike Johnson’s latest ploy to pass the Safeguarding American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act.

Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Director Russell Vought pressed House GOP lawmakers to take the initial steps in passing a new $95 billion budget bill that would include funding for Trump’s war with Iran, aid for U.S. farmers and additional money to states to carry out election integrity measures.

Trump has thrown his support behind the measure, but some fiscal hawks have been indicating as recently as Tuesday morning that they were concerned about the lack of budget cuts to offset the new federal spending.

TRUMP INTERVENES AS HOUSE GOP REVOLT PUTS SAVE AMERICA ACT AT CENTER OF SPENDING SHOWDOWN

But during their meeting, Vought told House Republicans that spending cuts could be sought via another budget bill to be completed after the current legislation, multiple people told Fox News Digital.

The SAVE America Act has been stalled in the Senate, where at least some Democrats are needed to join the GOP in breaking a filibuster to advance to a final vote. It passed the House in February.

The latest House GOP plan would pass the bill via the budget reconciliation process, which would skirt Democrats’ opposition by lowering the Senate’s threshold for advancement to just a simple majority. Republicans used the maneuver to pass Trump’s One Big, Beautiful Bill Act and add additional funding for immigration enforcement.

HOUSE REPUBLICANS UNLOCK RECONCILIATION PROCESS TO FUND ICE AND BORDER PATROL WITHOUT DEMOCRATS

A potential fourth budget bill would seek to cut spending by rooting out waste, fraud and abuse after the current bill is completed. Johnson has already been making a similar pitch to some House lawmakers, Fox News Digital was told Monday.

The first chamber-wide step in the reconciliation process is to pass a framework that directs the relevant committees to then craft details dealing with their allocated budgets. Those details are then put back into a single sweeping bill that must pass the House and Senate before getting to Trump’s desk for his signature.

Some fiscal hawks said during the meeting that they would support the legislation as long as it did not water down the SAVE America Act, which Vought indicated it would not, sources told Fox News Digital.

GOP'S $95B DEFENSE PACKAGE SURVIVES CRITICAL HURDLE AMID FISCAL HAWK REVOLT

"I’m focused on the SAVE America Act. It is the most important bill. I’m willing to eat some spending to get that across the line," Rep. Keith Self, R-Texas, told Fox News Digital. Asked if he was confident the reconciliation bill would get that accomplished, Self said, "That’s what we were told."

At least one House Republican, Rep. David Schweikert, R-Ariz., pressed Vought about his concerns about the increase in federal spending in the current legislation. Schweikert went as far as to say he was disgusted by the push to increase spending without cutting it elsewhere, Fox News Digital was told.

Vought responded that he had years of experience writing conservative Republican budgets and pointed out that Republicans had already achieved spending reductions during this term, Fox News Digital was told.

Other fiscal hawks also appeared to have lingering concerns.

Rep. Eric Burlison, R-Mo., who told Fox News Digital he was skeptical of the bill on Monday, indicated to reporters after the meeting that he had outstanding concerns.

"I spoke at the microphone. I said that right now I'm having a hard time, with that, with that bill, I want to have discussions if they need my vote," Burlison said. "I just wanted to let them know if you're doing a whip count right now, I wouldn't put me in the yes column…I'm not 100% convinced that the SAVE language is going to be held solid through the Senate. In fact, I would bet against it. I just, I don't have any confidence that the Senate will keep that language tight."

One source in the room told Fox News Digital that another fiscal hawk, Rep. Chip Roy, R-Texas, suggested he was inclined to vote for the budget framework.

Roy told Fox News Digital he was undecided, however.

"People wonder why the so-called 3AM caucus exists and why we hold our votes to the last minute to put pressure on Republicans in Congress to deliver? It’s because they revert to the mean — which is spending, amnesty, unlimited immigration and political show votes. I want FULL SAVE. I want this unpaid-for spending to be paid for in the 4th reconciliation bill. I want clarity on our war mission while we are backfilling munitions," he said.

Some Republicans are also unsure if they could complete a fourth reconciliation bill before the end of this year, given their slim margins.

One House GOP lawmaker, who was granted anonymity to speak freely, only texted Fox News Digital a GIF of Ryan Gosling stifling a laugh when asked if they believed another bill could be done.

Rep. Lloyd Smucker, R-Pa., told Fox News Digital, "I don’t know about 4.0," but added he would like to see more spending cuts.

The SAVE America Act would require proof of citizenship in the voter registration process and implementation of federal voter ID standards.

item.title: Silicon Valley developer accused of murder after investigators say Tesla reached 142 mph before fatal crash
item.description: A Tesla driver was allegedly traveling 142 mph before slamming into stopped traffic in San Jose, killing a mother and her 1-year-old daughter in a fiery crash.
item.content:encoded:
A Silicon Valley software developer and startup founder has been charged with murder after prosecutors say he drove his Tesla at more than 140 mph before plowing into stopped traffic on a California highway, triggering a fiery crash that killed a mother and her young daughter.

Zachary Chernicky, 31, has been arrested and charged with two counts of murder in connection with the Dec. 2, 2025, crash in San Jose, California.

The Santa Clara County District Attorney's Office alleges data recovered during the investigation shows Chernicky was driving 142 mph 15 seconds before impact, 126 mph five seconds before impact and still moving at about 102 mph when his Tesla slammed into a Lexus stopped in traffic.

'SLAP ON THE WRIST': ILLEGAL IMMIGRANT TRUCKER'S SENTENCE IGNITES OUTRAGE AFTER CRASH KILLED 3

Chernicky was arraigned Tuesday, ordered held without bail and is scheduled to return to court Aug. 25.

The California Highway Patrol (CHP) said the July 13 arrest followed a "comprehensive investigation" conducted in coordination with the Santa Clara County District Attorney's Office, adding that it hopes the arrest "represents an important step toward the justice the victims' family deserves as they continue to grieve."

"The law says that it is murder when someone shoots into a crowd," Santa Clara County District Attorney Jeff Rosen said in a statement. "When someone is driving 140 mph, then the driver is the shooter and their car is the bullet."

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

The Lexus burst into flames after being struck, killing front-seat passenger Ivana Balistreri, 29, and her 1-year-old daughter, Lilliana. KTVU in San Francisco reported that Lilliana was strapped into a child safety seat behind Balistreri. Lilliana died just two weeks before her second birthday.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Authorities said Balistreri's fiancé, who was driving the Lexus, survived the crash.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

CHP said Chernicky's Tesla also struck another Tesla and a Toyota. Three of the vehicles caught fire.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Prosecutors emphasized that the criminal case centers on Chernicky's alleged speed — not the vehicle he was driving.

SEND US A TIP

"I've seen some coverage of this incident that says things like, 'Tesla crash kills a family,' but that headline misses the point," Santa Clara County Deputy District Attorney Marisa McKeown said in remarks reported by KTVU. "The Tesla didn't cause this crash. Chernicky did by driving over 140 miles per hour."

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

A publicly available professional profile identifies Chernicky as the founder of CHURRDJ, a San Jose startup developing software for Pioneer DJ equipment. The profile states he previously worked in technical support roles at Apple, Gong, Glean and AI company Mistral AI.

Fox News Digital has reached out to Apple, Gong, Glean and Mistral AI to verify Chernicky's employment history and request comment, and to the Santa Clara County Public Defender's office.

CHP also used the case to warn motorists about the dangers of excessive speed.

"Do not speed. Speeding costs lives," the agency said. "CHP will hold reckless drivers accountable."

item.title: Connecticut Sun's road struggles face a tough test against a Fever team winning seven of its last 10
item.description: The Indiana Fever hold a 10-5 home record as Kelsey Mitchell leads scoring in eight of nine games, while the Connecticut Sun struggle at 7-19.
item.content:encoded:
Believe it or not, we are only two months away from the WNBA Playoffs. There is a lot of stuff still to come with the season, but when your season is only 44 games, it tends to go by fairly quickly. This has been an interesting campaign. With two new teams in the league, a big turnaround from the Wings, and somewhat of a falloff from the Aces and Liberty, there might be some new excitement in the postseason. Before we get there, let's talk about the Sun and the league's most popular team, the Fever.

The Connecticut Sun are 7-19 for the season and have won just three games on the road. I believe this will be the last season for the Sun in Connecticut, and this will be one that everyone forgets. The Sun started the year with just two wins in 15 games, but have gone 5-4 over the past nine games, so at least the Sun are showing a bit of progress. The biggest problem for this team seems to be that it can't find anyone to get a bucket when absolutely needed.

The team is led by Brittney Griner, but she is scoring just 14.1 points in 25.8 minutes. This is her second-lowest total point production since her rookie season. Last year was her worst, posting just 9.8 points per game. Perhaps it is because she is 35 years old and slowing down, but she is no longer the force she once was. The bigger issue for the team is that it is just a hodgepodge of players - some are castoffs from other teams, others are free agents that have been pieced together. However, the pieces really don't fit well.

CAITLIN CLARK SMACKED IN THE FACE BY WNBA LEGEND AFTER GETTING FOUL THAT COULD TRIGGER FINE IN BIG WIN

The Indiana Fever have a better record than the Sun, but that's not much of a challenge. I think one of the things to remember about the Fever is that they get the best effort from every team they face, every night. The WNBA players don't really like Caitlin Clark (at least that's how it seems), and the effort level is clearly different in games with her involved. The Fever are 10-5 at home this year, and have won seven of their past ten games overall. One other thing to remember about the Fever is that the beginning of this season was a major adjustment.

Clark was out almost all of last season, and her coming into the fold once again, even after a really successful run in the playoffs last year without her, meant the team had to learn new roles. She is ball-dominant and a great passer, so the team had to change its offense a bit. The Fever do seem to be getting back to what they were best at last season, when they looked to get Kelsey Mitchell buckets. She has led them in scoring in eight of the past nine games. Perhaps it is a balance between last year's success and this year's adjustments.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

The Sun are not a team that you'd expect to score a ton of points - averaging only about 80 points per game. However, the Fever are not a very good defensive team, and again, you get a big effort every time from the opposition. I feel like the total can go either way, I don't know that the Sun can stop the Fever, but I don't think the Fever have much interest in playing defense, either.

CAITLIN CLARK DROPS 32-POINT DOUBLE-DOUBLE IN TAYLOR SWIFT NIKES AS FEVER SURVIVE STUNNING SKY COMEBACK IN OT

The Fever have a rest advantage in this game; they've been at home for a while, but did have a back-to-back before this. Still, they've been off for a couple of days. Being in the same gym for multiple days helps with sight lines for shooters and allows them to be more comfortable. The Sun will have a long stretch off after this game, but will still be on the road. I like the Fever early in this one as I'm not sure they will blow out the Sun late. Connecticut has kept games close on the road lately. Give me the Fever 1st quarter at -2.5. I think this is where they will have the biggest advantage of being familiar for shooting.

item.title: Mace snubs Trump's preferred Senate candidate, backs Ralph Norman instead
item.description: Rep. Nancy Mace breaks from Trump to endorse Ralph Norman in South Carolina's special Senate election to replace the late Sen. Lindsey Graham.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: A key South Carolina Republican is breaking from President Donald Trump in the race to replace the late Sen. Lindsey Graham.

Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., is planning to endorse her colleague and former foe in the race to be South Carolina’s governor, Rep. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., in what is shaping up to be a messy snap election in the Palmetto State.

"This is it. A once-in-a-generation opportunity to send a tried-and-true conservative warrior to the United States Senate," Mace said in a statement to Fox News Digital. "Ralph Norman doesn't bend, doesn't break, and doesn't back down."

NANCY MACE REVEALS SENATE DECISION AS LINDSEY GRAHAM'S SISTER REDRAWS THE RACE

"He's fought the swamp, fought reckless spending, and fought for South Carolina at every turn," she continued. "If you want a proven conservative with a 100% conservative voting record, Ralph Norman is the man. I'm all in, and South Carolina should be too."

Her endorsement comes after she announced her decision not to run, and will come as the weeklong filing period for the special election, which is scheduled for Aug. 11, opens up. Mace isn’t the only lawmaker backing Norman’s candidacy, which he announced over the weekend after asking Trump for his endorsement.

Sens. Rick Scott, R-Fla., and Mike Lee, R-Utah, have also thrown their endorsement power behind him, noting their shared commitment to passing the SAVE America Act as a key factor in their support.

REPUBLICANS DEFY TRUMP, JUMP INTO SOUTH CAROLINA SENATE RACE DESPITE HIS HANDPICKED SUCCESSOR

Norman said in a statement to Fox News Digital that he was "honored," to score Mace’s endorsement in the race.

"Now more than ever, South Carolina needs a PROVEN conservative fighter who will stand with President Trump, advance the America First agenda, and help pass the SAVE America Act in the U.S. Senate," Norman said.

Meanwhile, Graham, who was considered to be a placeholder for the remainder of her late brother’s term, is all-in on running for a full, six-year stint in the Senate.

Trump, after meeting with her in the White House last week, threw his full support behind her, and urged, "run, Darline, run," in a Truth Social post shortly after their meeting.

Graham has so far scored endorsements from key allies in the Senate, including Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso, R-Wyo., who said, "Darline Graham is an excellent senator — she’ll continue to be an excellent senator, and she has my full support."

TIM SCOTT SAYS HE'D 'LOVE TO SEE' FOX NEWS HOST REPLACE LINDSEY GRAHAM

Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., however, is staying out of the race for now.

Thune said that Graham is a "strong candidate," and though she lacked elected experience, he noted she was "very accomplished in her own life."

"She has a right, like everybody else, to get in that race," Thune said.

Meanwhile, another South Carolina Republican, Rep. Russell Fry, is also launching a campaign.

Both Mace and Norman went after Fry on Monday, warning that his bid for the Senate could further shrink House Republicans’ razor-thin majority and tip the balance of power in favor of House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, D-N.Y., and Democrats.

Fry can appear for multiple offices on the ballot under South Carolina law, He was unopposed for his primary election for South Carolina’s 7th congressional district, and if he wins the nomination, he’ll be on the ballot twice for both.

Ultimately, he’ll have to choose if he wins both, which would trigger another special election to fill his place. That is not a problem for either Mace or Norman, who both ran and failed to clinch the Republican gubernatorial nomination last month.

"I will never hand Hakeem Jeffries that gift," Mace said on X.

"If you're running for two offices at once, you've already told us who you are," she continued. "That's not about politics anymore — that's about power. You're either with the people you serve or you're not."

item.title: Biden’s fight to keep Hur interview tapes under wraps suffers fresh court blow
item.description: A federal appeals court ruled against Joe Biden's bid to block audio recordings and transcripts from Robert Hur's classified documents probe.
item.content:encoded:
Former President Joe Biden’s emergency request to block the release of audio recordings and transcripts of his interviews with a memoir ghostwriter — materials obtained during special counsel Robert Hur’s classified-documents investigation — was rejected by a federal appeals court Monday.

"We conclude that any remaining incursion on personal privacy from disclosure of the now-redacted materials likely does not outweigh the public interest in disclosure," a divided three-judge panel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit wrote in its ruling.

The court kept an existing temporary block on disclosure in place through Aug. 3, allowing Biden time to seek further review.

The Justice Department obtained recordings of Biden's 2016 and 2017 conversations with Mark Zwonitzer, his ghostwriter for the memoir "Promise Me, Dad," and Hur’s team prepared transcripts during its investigation into Biden’s handling of classified information.

BIDEN SCORES TEMPORARY COURT VICTORY AS TRUMP-APPOINTED JUDGE DELAYS RELEASE OF HUR INVESTIGATION MATERIALS

Hur investigated Biden’s handling of classified documents dating from his time as vice president in the Obama administration and as a U.S. senator from Delaware. He declined to pursue criminal charges because he concluded prosecutors could not prove beyond a reasonable doubt that Biden willfully retained or disclosed the material, and said Biden could appear to a jury as a "sympathetic, well-meaning, elderly man with a poor memory."

The plaintiff, conservative think tank Heritage Foundation, has fought for over two years to retrieve the materials through FOIA requests, arguing the public has an interest in reviewing the recordings and transcripts that Hur based his decision on.

Biden’s attorneys have argued the conversations during the memoir interviews were personal and intended to remain private.

TRUMP LASHES OUT AT BIDEN OVER SUING DOJ TO HIDE INTERVIEW AUDIO FILES

His team has been fighting to keep the recordings under wraps and has noted Biden is now a private citizen not seeking public office.

Dissenting Judge Florence Pan, a Biden nominee, wrote that Biden "has shown a substantial privacy interest" in the conversations, The Associated Press reported.

Hur's report repeatedly referenced Biden's recorded conversations with Zwonitzer and described some exchanges as "painfully slow."

WATCH: BIDEN APPEARS CONFUSED ABOUT WHERE TO EXIT STAGE AFTER DEMOCRATIC GALA REMARKS

He said Biden at times struggled to recall events and relay information, observations that fueled scrutiny of Biden's cognitive abilities during an election year.

The Justice Department reversed its earlier position in February and said it intended to disclose redacted versions of the recordings and transcripts to the plaintiffs and the House Judiciary Committee.

Biden filed a lawsuit to stop the disclosure and claimed private conversations should remain protected under the Privacy Act.

Fox News reached out to Biden’s attorneys and the Heritage Foundation for comment.

Fox News Digital’s Elaine Mallon contributed to this report.

item.title: Red Sox come back from the dead, move into postseason contention with remarkable 14-game win streak
item.description: The Boston Red Sox have gone from surefire trade deadline sellers to occupying a postseason spot during their remarkable 14-game win streak.
item.content:encoded:
What a difference a few weeks can make.

The Boston Red Sox were headed for the American League basement, seemingly destined to be sellers at the trade deadline. Experts were already including starter Sonny Gray and relief ace Aroldis Chapman in mock trade proposals.

Then they won 14 games in a row. They went from 37-48 to 51-48, and now hold sole possession of third Wild Card spot in the American League.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

It’s hard to imagine these Red Sox capturing the hearts of Boston fans after headlines like these were being written in April: "The Boston Red Sox Are A Pathetic, Disgusting, Disgraceful Baseball Team."

But here they are, just one more victory away from tying the team’s longest winning streak in franchise history, at 15 games, which was set in 1946 with Ted Williams leading the charge. The Red Sox are trying to not think about what’s at stake.

"I’m trying not to, but I want it," third baseman Caleb Durbin said about the possibility of tying the club record. "I can’t speak for anyone else. We really haven’t talked about it, but I want to make history here."

"It would mean a lot, for sure," first baseman Willson Contreras said. "We are trying to play the same way every day. … If it happens, that would be great for us. We can celebrate that. If it doesn’t happen, you have to keep going."

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Durbin and Contreras both played major roles in the team’s come-from-behind 6-5 victory over the Baltimore Orioles on Monday night at Fenway Park.

With the Red Sox trailing 4-3 in the bottom of the sixth inning with a man on second base, Contreras pummeled Orioles starter Shane Baz’s 86-mph knuckle-curve off the Green Monster. Right fielder Wilyer Abreu raced home, tying the game at 4-4.

The Orioles responded in the top of the seventh inning when outfielder Tyler O’Neill grounded into a fielder’s choice, allowing Taylor Ward to score and giving Baltimore a 5-4 lead.

The Red Sox rallied in the bottom of the seventh inning when center fielder Ceddanne Rafaela singled through the left side of the infield with two outs, and shortstop Tsung-Che Cheng scored to tie it at 5-5.

CHICAGO CUBS FAN SLAPS YOUNGER FAN FOR NOT STANDING DURING NATIONAL ANTHEM IN VIRAL VIDEO

Durbin delivered the final blow to the Orioles in the eighth inning, hammering Tyler Wells’ 91-mph cutter 364 feet over the Green Monster, and sending the Fenway Faithful into a frenzy.

The Red Sox third baseman was euphoric as he rounded the bases.

"There’s not a lot of times that you black out – as they say,’’ Durbin said. "I think that was one of the moments where I didn’t know what was happening. I couldn’t tell you what I looked like running around the bases."

"I can’t imagine it being more electric than tonight," Durbin said. "It might be. Tonight was awesome, though."

Contreras was one of the first players to jump over the railing onto the field after Durbin’s homer.

"I felt like (the) playoffs, going to the World Series, to be honest," he said. "Hopefully it happens. We’re on the way, but there’s a lot of season left, a lot of games to be played."

The Red Sox hope the good vibes carry over into Tuesday, when they face the Orioles (49-52) in the second game of a three-game series at 7:10 p.m. ET, and look to etch their names alongside the legendary Williams.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Identifying three breakout SEC players who will be household names by season's end in 2026
item.description: From a former five-star Georgia flip at Vanderbilt to LSU's next great cornerback, these three relatively unknown SEC players could explode in 2026.
item.content:encoded:
We are right in the thick of SEC Media Days in Tampa, which means the 2026 college football season is just around the corner.

Fans can find comfort in knowing that in a little more than a month, their Saturday afternoons and evenings will be filled with the heart-stopping SEC action they've been craving all offseason.

In honor of this joyous occasion, I thought it would be good to identify three breakout players in the conference that you need to know heading into the 2026 season.

GREG SANKEY ADDRESSES SEC BREAKAWAY TALK AS MICHIGAN TRIES TO STEAL SPOTLIGHT WITH AD WARDE MANUEL DECISION

These are players that are relatively unknown to all but the most hardcore fans, but will be household names by season's end.

Let's get to know some of the next big things in the SEC, shall we?

Though several schools claim to hold the title of "DBU," the Tigers of LSU are one of the few programs that have a legitimate stake to that claim by churning out droves of NFL-ready corners and safeties over the years.

Sophomore D.J. Pickett might be the next in a long line of LSU cornerbacks to stamp his name in the annals of SEC lore, and it's easy to see why.

The 6'5" prodigy saw the field extensively as a true freshman, racking up 35 total tackles (28 solo) and three interceptions.

ED ORGERON-LANE KIFFIN PAIRING ALREADY PAYING DIVIDENDS FOR LSU TIGERS DURING PIVOTAL RECRUITING WEEKEND

If he keeps up with his growth trajectory, Pickett's second year should catapult him to legendary status, and the sky is truly the limit for this young man.

He has every physical tool you could possibly want in a modern day defensive back, with an insane wingspan to complement his 6'5" frame and legit sub-4.4 speed.

I fully expect Pickett to blossom under second-year defensive coordinator Blake Baker, and I can see an All-American selection in his future.

We must be living in a new age of college football when a Vanderbilt player makes a list like this, but that's just a testament to how improved the Commodores have been under Clark Lea and how special freshman quarterback Jared Curtis could be.

While "recruitniks" will no doubt recognize the name, I don't think any casual college football fan is familiar with just how special this kid can be.

That should change six months from now.

Curtis was a top-five player in the country and stunned the college football world when he flipped his commitment from Georgia to the hometown 'Dores, and that alone should give everyone in Nashville a reason for optimism.

If he's good enough for the recruiting juggernaut in Athens, he's good enough for everyone.

There's a reason Curtis is projected to be the starter without having ever taken a snap in college football, as the freshman phenom has the prototypical size (6'3" and 230 lbs) and arm strength to make every throw needed.

Offensive coordinator Tim Beck made the most out of former signal caller Diego Pavia, taking him from little-known New Mexico State transfer to Heisman finalist, and although it still remains to be seen whether that was more Pavia or Beck's doing, I expect similar success from the third-year OC now that he has his hands on a blue-chip prospect.

There will be growing pains, certainly. I don't expect an 18-year-old to come in and light the SEC on fire from day one and the schedule (as always) is unforgiving, but I definitely believe Curtis will improve throughout the season and become one of the best young passers in the conference by December.

One thing that all three players on this list have in common is that they are former consensus five-stars, the difference between the other two and Gators defensive end LJ McCray is that he has had a little more time in college than the other two.

That could be seen as both a good and bad thing.

On one hand, five-stars are generally supposed to be somewhat impactful from the first time they step on the field, which wasn't the case for McCray.

On the other hand, you could make the argument that a player like McCray has been seasoned in the weightroom and nutrition program and is ready to break out.

Florida fans and coaches are hoping it's the latter, as McCray hasn't lived up to his five-star billing in his two years of service.

That should change this fall, as reports out of Gainesville have been glowing regarding the former Daytona Beach prospect.

For starters, McCray has bulked up his 6'7" frame to 275 lbs of solid mass, and his numbers in the weightroom suggest these gains are mostly muscle.

That's big news, as one of McCray's biggest problems has been his inability to match up physically against some of the tackles in the SEC.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

Another thing working in his favor could be that he gets to start opposite Jayden Woods, who earned Freshman All-SEC honors in 2025 and was a hot commodity in the transfer portal before returning to Florida.

Woods will garner a lot of attention from offensive coordinators in 2026, leaving McCray free to dominate in one-on-one matchups.

If he puts his stellar offseason together with the opportunities presented on the field, this could be a monster year for McCray.

item.title: Long-hidden UFO information at center of Trump push to free former officials
item.description: Trump directs agencies to waive NDAs for former government employees and contractors willing to share UFO information through official channels.
item.content:encoded:
EXCLUSIVE: President Donald Trump has directed federal agencies to waive prior nondisclosure agreements (NDAs) for former government employees and contractors cooperating with unidentified anomalous phenomena investigations, Fox News Digital has learned.

"President Trump recently directed the Department of War and the intelligence community to allow former government employees or contractors, that have relevant information about the disclosure of UAP, to come forward to meet with designated government representatives — AARO or the President’s UAP taskforce, ‘PURSUE’ — and have their prior NDAs abrogated," a senior administration official said, with AARO referring to the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office, the Pentagon office established to investigate UFOs and other unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAPs).

Another senior administration official emphasized the move was not a "declassification directive" and only allowed employees to come forward through government channels.

Officials say years of congressional inquiries, whistleblower allegations and newly declassified records have raised questions about whether additional witnesses have remained silent because they believed prior nondisclosure agreements prevented them from cooperating with government investigations.

NEW TRUMP UFO FILE DUMP INCLUDES MILITARY FOOTAGE OF MYSTERIOUS STAR-SHAPED OBJECT

UFO WHISTLEBLOWER CLAIMS BILLIONS IN SECRET SPENDING HIDDEN FROM CONGRESS

"People who believe that they have information have expressed reluctance in coming forward," the official said. "We are eliminating that source of reluctance."

Like other government employees and defense contractors with access to classified information or sensitive national security programs, many witnesses would have signed nondisclosure agreements as a condition of receiving security clearances or access to restricted information. Those agreements generally prohibit unauthorized disclosure of classified or otherwise protected information and typically remain in effect even after an individual leaves government service.

Former officials and contractors who disclose protected information without authorization risk losing security clearances, facing civil penalties or, in some circumstances, criminal prosecution if classified information is improperly disclosed.

Under the new process, former government employees and contractors who believe they possess relevant UAP information would first meet with designated officials at the Pentagon's All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) or the president's UAP task force, PURSUE, who hold the appropriate security clearances. During those authorized discussions, any applicable nondisclosure agreements could be waived, allowing witnesses to share information without fear of violating prior agreements.

Information provided through the process would be reviewed through the administration's existing declassification procedures.

"Any information that is received that sheds light on the UAP mystery will ultimately be made public," the official said.

The official said the administration does not know whether potential witnesses actually possess significant new information, but wants to remove any perceived legal barriers so investigators can determine whether previously undisclosed information exists.

"You don't know what you don't know," the official said.

The official said the focus is on incidents that remain unexplained, rather than known foreign technology, classified U.S. programs or natural phenomena.

The latest directive builds on the Trump administration's broader effort to declassify decades of government records related to unidentified anomalous phenomena. Previous releases have included FBI and Pentagon records describing unexplained sightings reported by military personnel, law enforcement and astronauts, including accounts of glowing "mother orbs" that witnesses said released smaller objects near a sensitive U.S. national security site, mysterious orange spheres observed during military operations, and Apollo-era transcripts in which astronauts described unexplained lights and "jagged, angular fragments" drifting outside their spacecraft.

The latest tranche of UAP records included expanded details and imagery from a 2015 incident involving an unidentified object over the Pantex nuclear weapons facility in Texas, along with newly declassified infrared footage showing a mysterious six-pointed star-shaped object tracked by a U.S. military sensor over the Yellow Sea on Jan. 1, 2025.

The official said the next tranche of files will be made available "very soon."

The directive follows years of complaints from some lawmakers and whistleblowers that former officials with firsthand knowledge were reluctant to come forward because of secrecy obligations.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, then the vice chairman of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said in 2023 that multiple individuals with firsthand knowledge had approached the committee but were hesitant to speak publicly because of potential repercussions.

"Some of these people still work in the government and frankly, a lot of them are very fearful — fearful of their jobs, fearful of their clearances, fearful of their career, and some, frankly, are fearful of harm coming to them," he said on News Nation in June 2023.

"Grant immunity to anyone who has already come forward, and everyone who will come forward. Open the door and let them speak," Rep. Eric Burlison, R-Mo., demanded during a June 9 press conference on Capitol Hill.

Public interest in UAPs accelerated in 2017 after The New York Times revealed the Pentagon had quietly operated a secret program to investigate unexplained aerial encounters and published Navy cockpit videos showing objects exhibiting unusual flight characteristics.

Since then, Congress has held multiple bipartisan hearings on the issue, lawmakers have pressed intelligence agencies for greater transparency, and former intelligence officer David Grusch's allegations that the government had concealed information about UAP programs renewed calls for additional disclosure, despite the government's denial of his central claims.

During the same event, former Air Force intelligence officer Grusch renewed his allegations that secret UAP programs had operated outside normal congressional oversight, calling it "a real fraud, waste and abuse issue" and claiming his investigation uncovered "slush funds to the tune of billions of dollars per annum for these activities."

The Pentagon has disputed Grusch's central claims, and AARO has said its investigations found no verified evidence of extraterrestrial technology or credible evidence that the government has secretly recovered or reverse-engineered craft of non-human origin.

item.title: Framber Valdez and wind-blown Wrigley Field make Tigers vs Cubs under 8.5 a compelling MLB play
item.description: Framber Valdez faces David Peterson at Wrigley Field with wind blowing in, setting up a potential under as the Detroit Tigers visit the Chicago Cubs.
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday brought a loss on the Twins as I was backing the wrong horse in the race, apparently. The normally reliable Joe Ryan looked awful and allowed a bunch of runs early. I actually prefer to lose this way because I don't need to pay attention to it. It was the wrong read, and I can start looking at the next day of games. That was a game between two AL Central teams; I'm going to try one with another from the division as the Tigers take on the Cubs.

The Detroit Tigers are still below .500, but no one in the division has run away with the lead, so there is still a chance they can turn it around. You don't need to give them a big reminder about how August and September can change a division race. They were massive favorites last year to win the division, and the Guardians had a phenomenal stretch to steal the division. The Tigers got the last laugh as they won in the playoffs. Still, this team needs a jolt in one way or another, and all the talk surrounding them is about if Tarik Skubal will be traded or not.

During the offseason, they knew they would have to come to a decision about Skubal, and they signed Framber Valdez, the best available starter, to kind of soften the blow of losing Skubal. For the year, Valdez is 5-6 with a 4.10 ERA and a 1.32 WHIP. Those numbers aren't terrible, but they also aren't exactly top-of-the-rotation quality, either. Valdez has done a pretty good job of keeping the ball down, overall. He has 178 ground balls as opposed to 158 fly balls, and that doesn't count his strikeouts. Cubs hitters haven't done particularly well against Valdez, either, batting just 4-for-32 against him.

The Chicago Cubs are basically in the same spot they were last season. They were solid as a club, but they are still looking up at the Milwaukee Brewers. They are one of the streakier clubs in baseball, and if their offense starts to slow down, they are really going to struggle. That's mostly because the pitching staff seems to be held together by duct tape and Band-Aids. They've made a couple of trades to get some arms that might help them for the year, but no one you really want to go into the postseason with.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

One of those trades they made was for today's starter, David Peterson. His numbers are pretty ugly, but the Cubs either thought they could turn him around or saw something in him that made them think it was a worthwhile investment. He is 4-7 with a 6.45 ERA and a 1.64. He was much better in his last outing for them than his first. Against the Orioles, he went five innings and allowed two hits and one earned run. Against the Cardinals, he allowed 10 earned runs on nine hits over 3.2 innings. The Tigers are 3-for-8 against him.

Some ballparks have a bigger impact on betting than others. When the wind is blowing out at Wrigley Field, the ball flies out, and it seems like the total can't be high enough. Tonight, the wind will be blowing in. Peterson is more of a fly ball pitcher, but there shouldn't be too much damage against him, considering the wind can knock stuff down. Valdez is more of a ground ball pitcher. I think this game will go under the 8.5 total tonight thanks to both pitchers being aided by the wind. The Tigers also aren't a major offensive force to begin with. Give me the under.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

For more sports betting information and plays, follow David on X/Twitter: @futureprez2024

item.title: Jalen Brunson says Knicks haven't discussed White House visit despite owner saying they were going
item.description: Jalen Brunson says the New York Knicks haven't discussed a White House visit as a team despite James Dolan's announcement that the invitation was accepted.
item.content:encoded:
New York Knicks owner James Dolan may have jumped the gun when he said his championship-winning squad would be White House bound.

Days after winning the team's first title in 53 years, Dolan announced that the team "receive[d] an invitation from the White House, which we accepted."

"Still have to figure out the details, but, yes, of course, I invited the president to come down to the game, right? He is a friend. I've known him for 30 years, and I'm very proud to bring the team to the White House," Dolan said at the time.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Well, Jalen Brunson is holding the horses.

"That’s something that we actually haven’t discussed as a team," Brunson told Variety in a recent interview. "And so, obviously, people are making assumptions and saying everything, but together we haven’t discussed it yet. And so we’ll cross our bridge when we get to it."

The Knicks would be the first NBA team to visit Trump in either of his terms, a note Brunson found "interesting."

President Donald Trump attended the Knicks' Game 3 loss to the San Antonio Spurs at Madison Square Garden. It turned out to be the only loss for the Knicks, who went on to win the NBA Finals in five games.

KNICKS CHAMPION JORDAN CLARKSON CAUGHT IN TRAVIS SCOTT NIGHTCLUB CHAOS AFTER STEPPING IN

Trump criticized the NBA for being "highly political" in 2020. NBA players were given the option to wear social justice messages on their jerseys, and numerous players knelt for the national anthem. During his trip to the Finals, Trump said basketball was "a little left-wing" but "great entertainment."

Knicks star Josh Hart has been critical of Trump in the past.

Dolan and New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani got into a war of words during the Finals regarding watch parties. Dolan then appeared to take a shot at Mamdani during the team's victory celebration at City Hall and did his best to stiff him in a photo op.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

The Dolan-Mamdani beef is hardly new. The spat began during the election cycle when Mamdani used the Knicks logo for his campaign, and the team issued a cease-and-desist letter. The White House then used the Knicks logo in a post shortly after last year's election to say, "Trump Is Your President." The Knicks reached out to the White House, which then removed the post.

"The Knicks remain neutral on political matters," the team said in a statement at the time. "We hope all our elected officials, whether current or recently elected, do a great job in office."

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Trump admin pausing over $1B in Medicaid payments to Minnesota, California over fraud, compliance concerns
item.description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. says California and Minnesota must prove Medicaid payments were legitimate before frozen federal funding will be released.
item.content:encoded:
The Trump administration is freezing more than $1 billion in federal Medicaid funding to California and Minnesota over suspected fraud, with HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. warning Tuesday that states won't receive another taxpayer dollar until they prove the payments were legitimate.

"If Governor Gavin Newsom or Governor Tim Walz wants this funding released, all they have to do is provide basic documentation showing that these services are legitimate and not fraudulent. And that's common sense," Kennedy said Tuesday during a press conference on the announcement.

The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) is withholding roughly $867 million from California and more than $200 million from Minnesota while federal officials review what they described as high-risk Medicaid claims and documentation deficiencies.

TREASURY INTERCEPTS NEARLY $99M IN FEDERAL PAYMENTS TO DECEASED INDIVIDUALS UNDER TRUMP FRAUD ORDER

Kennedy said the Trump administration is using artificial intelligence, advanced analytics and traditional financial reviews to identify suspicious spending, adding that states seeking federal Medicaid funding must demonstrate the payments comply with federal law.

"We are not sending Medicaid and Medicare dollars out the door until we have confidence that they are being spent lawfully and appropriately," Kennedy said.

CMS Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz said the administration's review uncovered repeated irregularities in both states, including claims submitted for deceased beneficiaries, providers previously flagged through fraud detection systems and spending patterns federal officials said were significant outliers.

"If it smells like fraud, we're not paying for it anymore," Oz said.

According to CMS, California accounted for the largest share of the funding freeze, with roughly $646 million tied to in-home supportive services and another $221 million associated with claims involving individuals with what Oz described as "unsatisfactory immigration status."

Oz said California's spending on in-home supportive services increased 24% over the past two federal fiscal years — roughly double the national average — and said the state has yet to adequately document hundreds of millions of dollars in claims.

WATCH: DR. OZ SAYS NEWSOM, OTHER BLUE STATES HAVE TURNED MEDICAID FRAUD INTO A ‘FEATURE’

In Minnesota, officials said the state was asked to review providers participating in 14 high-risk Medicaid service categories, including personal care and home-based services. Oz said Minnesota recently disenrolled roughly 3,000 providers after failed background checks, failed site visits and other compliance issues, prompting CMS to review nearly $200 million in claims tied to those providers.

Federal officials also cited millions of dollars in claims billed for services allegedly provided to beneficiaries after their deaths, calling such payments a major red flag.

Kennedy also announced that HHS is expanding exclusion authority, allowing both CMS and the HHS Office of Inspector General to remove individuals and entities found to have committed fraud from federal healthcare programs, with some offenders facing permanent bans.

Kennedy sharply criticized the Biden administration's oversight of Medicaid, arguing former HHS Secretary Xavier Becerra weakened anti-fraud efforts by reducing program integrity staffing and allowing questionable payments to continue while attempting to recover the money later.

"The scammers got paid. The taxpayers got stuck with these enormous bills," Kennedy said.

The administration said California and Minnesota can receive the withheld funding if they provide documentation showing the payments were legitimate.

"We have a duty to stop the payments, demand answers, and then follow the evidence wherever it leads," Kennedy said.

item.title: Cincinnati Reds pitcher rips 'f—ing fata–' umpire for getting in defender's way on ground ball
item.description: Caleb Ferguson's profane outburst at umpire Bill Miller followed a botched play where the ump blocked Edwin Arroyo's path to the ball in the seventh.
item.content:encoded:
Things are just continuing to go from bad to worse for the Cincinnati Reds.

After starting the season with a 20-12 record, they are just 25-43 in their last 68 games, and they were handed an 8-0 loss to the Seattle Mariners on Monday night.

But the Reds suffered an absolute facepalm moment in the bottom of the seventh inning, which had already gone awry thanks to Cal Raleigh's grand slam.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

However, the Reds didn't get much help afterward. Mariners outfielder Victor Robles hit a grounder to short, and second baseman Edwin Arroyo tried to make a play. However, he found umpire Bill Miller in his way, and the ball squeaked into the outfield while also getting past shortstop Elly De La Cruz.

Relief pitcher Caleb Ferguson was not happy, shouting obscenities at the umpire and ripping him for his appearance.

"F—ing fata–! Out of the way motherf—er," Ferguson, who had just allowed the Raleigh grand slam, appeared to yell at Miller.

THREE OF THE WORST-RATED UMPIRES IN MAJOR LEAGUE BASEBALL HAVE ONE THING IN COMMON

Seattle then had runners on the corners, but the Reds stopped the bleeding.

Reds manager Terry Francona ordered Ferguson to intentionally walk Dominic Canzone to load the bases and face Raleigh, but the decision totally backfired.

The slumping catcher, who finished in second place in last year's AL Most Valuable Player voting with his 60-homer season, is hitting just .163 this season, but he pounced on the first pitch and turned on a hanging breaking ball, sending it 408 feet into the left-field bleachers to break the game open. It was just his 10th home run of the season.

"I know what Raleigh’s hitting and, he still worries me," the veteran skipper said with a laugh. "But, I thought that was our best chance to have them not score. We could play the infield double-play depth. And, the worst possible outcome happened."

Seattle jumped into the AL West lead with the victory, which also brought them to 51-50 on the season. They are trying to find similar success after reaching Game 7 of last year's American League Championship Series.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: ‘Misleading voters’: Ossoff warns donors he’s falling behind despite leading Senate fundraising
item.description: Jon Ossoff claims his fundraising is faltering in urgent emails to supporters, despite raising $68.7 million and holding $42.6 million in cash on hand.
item.content:encoded:
Sen. Jon Ossoff is warning supporters that his campaign is falling behind and needs more money to keep its ads on the air, despite having one of the largest campaign war chests in the country.

The Georgia Democrat has raised $68.7 million since 2025, slightly more than Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico, the next highest fundraiser, who brought in $65.9 million after launching his campaign in September 2025. While securing massive donation hauls, Ossoff’s campaign has simultaneously told prospective donors that it needs money to keep ads running and that it is consistently behind on its fundraising goals.

In July alone, the Ossoff campaign — which the most recent available disclosures show to have $42.6 million in cash on hand — sent multiple emails to supporters claiming that it urgently needs money to keep its advertising push going.

FOX NEWS POLL: AN EARLY LOOK AT THE GEORGIA SENATE RACE

"$5 to keep us on the air," the subject line of a July 2 email reads, according to archive records. 

"And the more donations we receive right now, the further we can spread our message to persuade and turn out the voters we need to win big in November," the body of the email reads. "So I need your help — not just to keep this ad on the air, but to spread our message even further. Will you contribute $5 or whatever you can afford to keep our ad on the air and defeat Trump puppet, Mike Collins?"

A campaign spokeswoman told Fox News Digital when asked about the fundraising that Ossoff faces an extraordinary level of Republican spending because he is the only Democratic senator seeking re-election in a state President Donald Trump won in 2024.

"As the only Democratic Senator up for re-election in a state Trump won in 2024, Senator Ossoff is already facing millions of dollars of Republican spending against him, including an initial $44 million from the leading Republican Super PAC and countless more to come from Republican dark money groups empowered by a recent Supreme Court decision that allows them to open the floodgates for unlimited sums of money to prop up candidates, including scandal-ridden Mike Collins," the spokeswoman said.

WATCH: CORY BOOKER UNLEASHES FIERY CALL FOR 'FOOT SOLDIERS' AT MICHIGAN DEM CONFERENCE

Google ad records show that Ossoff is spending millions of dollars on messaging in states besides Georgia — including early presidential primary states such as Iowa and South Carolina as well as swing states like Pennsylvania and Michigan. The behavior, which is abnormal for Democrats in high-profile Senate races, is fueling speculation that Ossoff may launch a run for the presidency in 2028.

In a June 18 email, Ossoff's campaign told supporters that "it’s looking like we’re on pace to miss our fundraising goals for the month." The campaign made similar claims in April and March, according to archived emails.

Despite these lamentations, a short time later, the campaign said it has an "unstoppable grassroots momentum" after its fundraising numbers were publicly released.

"Jon Ossoff is misleading voters as he prepares to ditch Georgia for a shot at the White House," National Republican Senatorial Committee press secretary Bernadette Breslin told Fox News Digital. "But no amount of out-of-state ad spending can erase his failed record — from enabling Joe Biden’s reckless spending agenda to supporting open-border policies that allowed Laken Riley’s killer to roam free."

DEMOCRATS BUILD MIDTERM MOMENTUM, BUT REPUBLICANS STILL IN DRIVER'S SEAT IN SENATE MAJORITY BATTLE

While Ossoff has raised over 10 times more than GOP Senate nominee Rep. Mike Collins has this year, national Republican committees, such as the NRSC, have brought in significantly more cash than their Democratic counterparts.

These national committees have already pledged tens of millions of dollars to defeating Ossoff. Outside groups spent over $170 million against Ossoff and in support of his GOP opponent during the 2020 general and 2021 runoff elections in Georgia, leaving some expecting a similarly expensive race in 2026.

A recent Supreme Court case has also granted political parties greater leeway in coordinating their spending with individual candidates, which has been characterized as a victory for Republicans.

JON OSSOFF SILENT ON SPLC INDICTMENT AFTER TAKING MORE THAN $700K FROM AFFILIATE OF INDICTED GROUP

The fundraising emails reviewed by Fox News Digital for this story appear in the Archive of Political Emails, a project run by the right-of-center Defending Democracy Together Institute. 

Screenshots of fundraising texts from the Ossoff campaign have also circulated on social media, attracting criticism from some given the significant financial resources already available to it.

GEORGIA REPUBLICANS HAMMER OSSOFF OVER SILENCE AFTER ICE DETAINER IN ALLEGED CHILD RAPE CASE

"This Ossoff mf texted me two days ago that he had no money," one anonymous X user posted, responding to a screenshot showing the nearly $100 million he has raised since 2021. The user then posted a screenshot of a text message they reportedly received from the Ossoff campaign where it claimed to be behind on its fundraising goals.

"Jon you have a bajillion dollars what are you talking about," the user texted back.

"He was BEGGING too," another user responded, posting a picture of a separate reported fundraising text they received from Ossoff’s campaign.

Beyond emails to supporters, the Ossoff campaign has also publicly complained that it needs more money.

Most polls for Georgia’s Senate election, including those funded by Republicans, show Ossoff leading Collins.

item.title: Joe Biden mandated all new vehicles come installed with a 'kill switch.' This Republican wants to repeal it
item.description: Rep. Scott Perry is pushing the No Kill Switches in Cars Act to repeal a federal mandate requiring passive driver impairment monitoring technology in new vehicles, citing privacy and cost concerns.
item.content:encoded:
Rep. Scott Perry, R-Penn., wants to kill the kill switch.

A bill Perry is seeking to push through Congress would rescind a provision in former President Joe Biden's Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act that mandates all new cars to come preinstalled with technology that ostensibly monitors whether a driver's blood alcohol levels are above the legal limit and can shut their vehicle off remotely.

"If you don't know what a kill switch is," Perry explained in an interview with Fox News Digital, "it's exactly what it sounds like. It kills your vehicle. The vehicle itself decides whether it appreciates and approves of your driving based on a series of algorithms, computer metrics, etcetera, about how it thinks you should be driving. And if you're not comporting with that, it shuts the vehicle off."

The two-paragraph No Kill Switches In Cars Act, shorter than the law it seeks to repeal, would nix the Biden-era provision that mandates all new cars contain technology which "monitors a driver's performance to identify driver impairment" and which "must also prevent or limit vehicle operation if the system determines the driver may be intoxicated or impaired."

Perry argued the law was an encroachment on the civil liberties of Americans, and that it would violate the Constitution's Fourth Amendment and make vehicles unnecessarily expensive.

DAVID MARCUS: WHY I'LL TAKE MY CLUNKER OVER ANY NEW CAR WITH A GOVERNMENT KILL SWITCH

"Cars are expensive and one of the reasons they're expensive is because we have all these systems on them. I'm a person that uses my mirrors to back up," Perry explained.

"I've never had an accident backing up or parking, but yet, if I buy a new car, I don't have an option whether I get a camera in the car or not. Maybe I want a new car, but I can't afford the camera and the lane assist, and now all these other features that are in the car monitoring me. I'm not allowed to buy that car. My goodness, this is America. We should be able to buy what we want. If you can't afford that stuff or don't want that stuff, it should be your choice.

Perry explained that he was all for efforts to mitigate drunk and impaired driving, even admitting that he's had family members affected by the phenomenon, but explained that the Biden-era provision was "a bridge too far."

TRUMP ADMIN INVESTIGATING 75 TRUCK DRIVER SCHOOLS FOR FRAUD, HELPING NON-CITIZENS GET LICENSES

"We've had impaired driving incidents within my own family. So we're well aware of the consequences, the pain, and the potential bad, catastrophic results of driving impaired. Whether it's drinking or something else, we get that. And that's why I think that we are supportive of other measures and enforcement of those measures, but not trying to seek to punish every single person that drives a car for the sake of the few that abuse that."

Perry imagined a number of scenarios in which a kill switch could improperly result in a driver who wasn't under the influence of alcohol losing access to their vehicle. 

"My wife was pregnant at one time, and we did leave to go deliver the baby. And I can just tell you, as a guy that's had a pregnant wife, they're very intensively interested in getting to the hospital in a timely fashion," Perry joked wryly.

"And I just picture somebody that they're trying to, maybe they're trying to get themselves to the hospital, maybe they are the husband, or the father of the child is not around, and they're driving differently than they have been in the past and the car just shuts off. Or another circumstance that I've envisioned where some young lady is leaving a store or some establishment and someone is following her, and she's scared, and she decides, 'Well, I think I'm just going to go to the police station. I've got to find the police station.' So while you're driving, You're trying to look it up. Where is it? And the car decides you're driving erratically and shuts off, that's the worst time for the car to shut off," he said.

"I live in central Pennsylvania, but it's hot in Arizona right now. I've been there. There are long stretches of highway. It's over 100 degrees. Your car decides to shut off, and you're left walking out in 100 degrees, maybe you're impaired, maybe the heat affects you physically. And, you know, there's a million scenarios that haven't been envisioned right now, where Americans' rights are being taken away, and the nanny state is forcing itself upon us."

"The intentions are fine," he continued. "We all want to stop drunk driving. But you don't punish everybody else for the sake of the few that violate the law. We don't do that in the United States of America, but yet we're choosing to do it here."

To Perry, the kill switch law is not simply about mitigating drunk driving, but indicative of a broader encroachment of corporate and biometric surveillance into the civil liberties of Americans everywhere.

DUFFY UNLEASHES ON BUTTIGIEG WITH SWIPE OVER WHICH ADMIN HAS ACCOMPLISHED MORE: 'MOVED LIKE A SLOTH'

"Couple it with all the other things that are happening at the same," Perry said. "Consider artificial intelligence, with facial recognition software, with gait analysis... with central bank digital currency," he continued. "One of the major car companies just applied for patents to monitor while you're driving your pupils, your respiration, your breathing, your heart rate, your conversation... and then the censorship of people online for their opinions. All that is part of the same conversation. I took an oath to uphold and defend the Constitution. And the constitution says that American citizens are not subject to unlawful search and seizure, and I think that this is a pretty good indication that there is unlawful search."

Perry again noted that "nobody is for drunk driving" but argued that mitigation efforts should be focused on enforcing existing laws and cooperating with law enforcement.

"What we should be for is punishing those and dealing with those who choose to drive impaired or unwittingly do it, but you don't punish everybody else for the sake of the few that do. I happen to not drink at all. So why should I… why should anybody have their privacy monitored and interrupted without a warrant, but also be forced to pay for it?" Perry asked.

'SLAP ON THE WRIST': ILLEGAL IMMIGRANT TRUCKER'S SENTENCE IGNITES OUTRAGE AFTER CRASH KILLED 3

Though Biden's bill required the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) to codify the law in its vehicle manufacturing regulation code, the agency missed a 2024 deadline, in part due to the logistical challenges of a lack of available monitoring technology.

A spokesperson for the agency told Fox News Digital that NHTSA is currently evaluating public comments on the agency's research into monitoring and into the rule. 

The spokesperson noted in particular a line in the agency's 2024 report to Congress which cast doubt on the immediate availability of passive impairment monitoring technology. 

"Of the technologies reviewed, most were in an early state of research and development or were not designed for in-vehicle use. Though the two 2024 reports documented some promising technologies, neither report found commercially available technology that detects driver alcohol impairment accurately and passively," the report read.

"Currently, detection technology around the legal limit continues to have an error rate that would be unacceptably high," the report also read.

Though Perry has picked up a growing number of GOP lawmakers to support his bill — 21 lawmakers have joined the original 14 introducing members as co-sponsors — advocacy organizations like Mothers Against Drunk Driving (MADD) disagree entirely with the premise of the term "kill switch."

"MADD does not support government surveillance, remote vehicle control or shutdowns, GPS tracking, or the collection, sharing, or sale of personal driving data," the organization told Fox News Digital in a statement. 

"The law directs the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration to develop a federal vehicle safety standard through an open, transparent rulemaking process that must include public input, rigorous testing and strong privacy safeguards. Once fully implemented, the law will save more than 10,000 lives every year. Americans should not have to choose between protecting individual privacy and preventing drunk driving. We can, and should, accomplish both. In 2024 alone, 11,904 people lost their lives to this entirely preventable crime. We believe Americans deserve every reasonable protection available to stop drunk driving tragedies while protecting privacy and civil liberties. Unnecessary delays based on inaccurate descriptions of what the law actually requires only prolong the devastating toll drunk driving continues to take on families and communities across the country," the statement concluded.

item.title: Nancy Guthrie video may have been erased after FBI kept out for days, director says
item.description: FBI Director Kash Patel says the four-day delay in federal involvement may have cost crucial Nest doorbell footage that was on the verge of deletion.
item.content:encoded:
EXCLUSIVE: The delay in allowing the FBI to assist in the investigation into Nancy Guthrie's suspected abduction may have cost crucial video evidence due to the elaborate process involved in recovering Nest doorbell footage from the crime scene when the victim did not have a cloud subscription and her physical camera was missing as well, according to the FBI director.

Guthrie, the mother of "Today" co-host Savannah Guthrie, was taken from her Tucson, Arizona, home in the early hours of Feb. 1, according to authorities.

The FBI worked with Google to recover cached video from Guthrie's Nest camera that was not intended for long-term storage and was on the verge of deletion, Director Kash Patel told Fox News Digital.

If there was more, it was lost before the FBI became involved, and its evidentiary value remains unknown.

NANCY GUTHRIE SHERIFF SCOLDED COUNTY OFFICIAL FOR SHARING TIP WITH FBI, SHE CLAIMS IN INTERVIEW

"We were looking for the needle of all needles in all haystacks, because since she didn't pay for it, they didn't record that data," he said. "But there was a chance that it was located in their cache just before it was being deleted."

That's where investigators found the video and still images that Patel made public on Feb. 10, in the hope that someone would recognize the suspect and turn him in.

A short time later, FBI experts had generated a rough physical description. The masked suspect is described as between 5 feet, 9 inches and 5 feet, 10 inches tall with a medium build. He was wearing a black Ozark Trail backpack.

NANCY GUTHRIE CASE: FORMER COP SHOWS HOW MISSING MOM COULD VANISH JUST YARDS FROM HOMES

A masked man appeared on Guthrie's Nest camera at least twice — once on the day of her Feb. 1 abduction, and once about three weeks earlier, Fox News Digital has reported previously. Authorities have not publicly ruled out the potential for additional suspects in the case.

There is only a single still image publicly available from the earlier visit. Experts say it's likely that video existed at one point.

If the FBI were involved sooner, agents may have been able to recover additional evidence, which potentially may have included a suspect vehicle, more footage of the masked intruder or even additional imagery from a prior visit to Guthrie's home, Patel said.

NANCY GUTHRIE SHERIFF DEFENDS PACE OF INVESTIGATION MORE THAN 4 MONTHS INTO SEARCH

"What we do know is that the longer you wait, there's no chance of recovering the video," said Morgan Wright, the CEO and founder of the National Center for Open and Unsolved Cases.
